title: Align repo/package versions with v0.8.1 release
description: The latest published GitHub release is tagged v0.8.1-live-coach, but repo/package/app version sources still say 0.8.0. Bump all first-class version sources to 0.8.1, rebuild affected JS artifacts, repack the MCPB, and attach the refreshed package to the live-coach release so release metadata and downloadable artifacts align.

description: Follow-up to minutes-nce. The MCP server still validates get_meeting/add_note/resource paths against its own MEETINGS_DIR env/default while list_meetings shells out to the CLI and uses Config::load().output_dir. Add a small machine-readable CLI command to expose the effective config (at minimum output_dir), then update MCP to cache/use that value for meeting-path validation when the CLI is available. Add regression coverage for the CLI-backed list/get split with a non-default output_dir.

The MCP server currently validates file paths against process.env.MEETINGS_DIR without expanding /Users/silverbook, /Users/silverbook, or ~, while list_meetings can still return real absolute paths via the CLI. Normalize configured roots before allowlist checks and add regression tests for get_meeting/path validation behavior.

title: Polish dictation HUD and cue feedback
description: Refine the working standard dictation shortcut experience with an elegant bottom-corner overlay, immediate visual state feedback, and better start/stop/success/error cue behavior so the feature feels intentional and trustworthy.
close_reason: Polished the dictation HUD with a bottom-corner transparent overlay, calmer state transitions, and a refined cue pack with reduced cue spam.

title: Rebuild raw dictation hotkeys as an advanced subsystem

Keep the standard shortcut as the primary product path while the raw-key path is rebuilt as advanced/experimental.

Make key capture more robust for modifier/special keys, persist enabled state in config, and restore the native hotkey monitor on startup.

title: Make hotkey diagnostic use LaunchServices app launch
description: The built-in hotkey probe returns a false negative when the app executable is invoked directly from the shell, even after Accessibility/Input Monitoring are granted. The same probe succeeds when the app is launched via LaunchServices (). Update the dev workflow and docs to run the diagnostic through app launch plus file output instead of direct executable invocation.
close_reason: Switched desktop hotkey diagnostics to a LaunchServices app launch path and updated the dev workflow/docs to avoid direct shell false negatives.

title: Make desktop dev install workflow contributor-safe

close_reason: Removed maintainer-specific signing defaults from local scripts and documented contributor-safe desktop dev setup for the open-source repo.

title: Add Windows support for the Minutes Tauri desktop app
description: Implement a credible first-pass Windows build for the Tauri desktop app. Focus on compiling and packaging the app on Windows, degrading macOS-only features cleanly, making core desktop flows work cross-platform (open file/URL, clipboard, notifications, tray actions, settings), and documenting what remains intentionally macOS-only for now. Coordinate with existing Windows CLI/core support instead of duplicating it.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Windows CI builds the Tauri desktop app or otherwise proves the desktop target on Windows. 2. macOS-only desktop features degrade cleanly on Windows instead of failing via osascript/open/pbcopy assumptions. 3. Core desktop flows (record/transcribe/search/assistant/settings/open artifact) work with Windows-safe code paths. 4. Docs/release messaging clearly distinguish Windows desktop support from macOS-only features. 5. Remaining gaps are tracked in follow-up beads if not completed.
close_reason: Completed

comments.author: silverstein
comments.text:
Implementation plan for `minutes-icn.1`:

1. Audit and harden all desktop code paths that assume macOS shell tools or APIs.
   - Replace direct `open` usage for files and URLs with cross-platform Tauri shell opens.
   - Replace or gate notification, clipboard, and paste automation paths that currently rely on `osascript` / `pbcopy`.
   - Gate tray actions and startup loops whose behavior is explicitly macOS-only.

2. Make platform-specific desktop features degrade cleanly on Windows.
   - Hide or relabel macOS-only settings and tray actions in the frontend.
   - Keep native dictation hotkey unsupported off macOS, but make the UI honest and non-alarming.
   - Suppress calendar/call-detection affordances when they are unavailable instead of showing dead UI.

3. Adjust packaging and startup for Windows desktop builds.
   - Remove unconditional bundling assumptions around the macOS `mic_check` helper.
   - Add Windows-oriented Tauri release/build workflow coverage alongside current CLI release coverage.
   - Keep macOS release behavior intact.

4. Update docs and release messaging.
   - Document current Windows desktop scope and known macOS-only features.
   - Update README/release guidance so users are not told the desktop app is macOS-only once the Windows build path exists.

5. Verify and land.
   - Run targeted Rust checks/tests and frontend sanity checks on this machine.
   - Use CI/build configuration as the source of truth for Windows desktop validation where local macOS cross-checking is inconclusive.
   - File follow-up beads for any remaining gaps discovered during implementation.

comments.author: silverstein
comments.text:
Implemented first-pass Windows desktop support for the Tauri app.

- Moved desktop notifications to a cross-platform path: macOS still uses native notification scripting, other platforms fall back to a native dialog instead of silent failure.
- Gated macOS-only tray/startup features (`calendar` refresh, call detection, tray copy/paste automation) so Windows does not expose dead UI or shell assumptions.
- Fixed frontend folder-opening logic to handle Windows paths instead of slicing only on `/`.
- Split Tauri config so `mic_check` and macOS bundle settings live in `tauri.macos.conf.json` rather than the shared config.
- Added Windows desktop CI coverage and a release workflow that attaches a desktop `minutes-desktop-windows-x64.exe` binary to tagged releases.
- Updated README/release docs to describe the Windows desktop scope honestly.

That can leave the hotkey state machine thinking a capture is active and produce incorrect stop/discard behavior on later releases. Clear hotkey runtime on early start failure.

title: Reference adapters v2: identity, idempotency, pins, tests
description:
Codex adversarial review of minutes-e7pc (2026-04-20) flagged the v1 adapters as a workable baseline with real v2 upgrades needed. The must-fix bugs (footgun cleanup cypher, broken cd sequence, glob vs rglob, MEETINGS_DIR env, path leak) were addressed in the e7pc ship. This bead covers the design-level work Codex flagged as deferred:

1. Identity-aware ingestion.

Current adapters treat the whole corpus as a single user_id / group_id. The frontmatter schema already exposes canonical slugs via `people[]` and structured display names via `attendees[]`. v2 should:
- Mem0: split memories per attendee using `people[]` slugs as user_id, with a fallback group identity for the corpus. Memories about Priya land under user_id=priya; memories about shared decisions land under a corpus-level identity.
- Graphiti: pre-seed canonical person nodes from `people[]` before pushing episodes. Guarantees the extractor clusters identity correctly instead of hoping names match across meetings.

2. Idempotency manifest.

Sidecar file (e.g. `.minutes-adapter-manifest.json` inside the meetings dir or in ~/.minutes/adapters/) keyed on (meeting_path, mtime, adapter_name). Skip meetings already pushed at the same mtime. On a real corpus this is the difference between "demo once" and "safe to run nightly."

3. Exact version pins.

Replace floor-only requirements.txt with pinned versions (mem0ai==X.Y.Z, graphiti-core==A.B.C) chosen against a known-good test run. Include a dated comment so future updates are easy.

4. Smoke tests.

At minimum a CI job that runs both adapters with --dry-run against tests/eval/fixtures/meetings/ and asserts the output line counts match expectations. Catches schema drift or adapter bit-rot.

5. Graphiti simpler backend.

Neo4j+OpenAI is a steep first-run bar. Investigate:
- FalkorDB (lighter, Docker one-liner)
- Ollama local embeddings instead of OpenAI (graphiti supports custom LLM clients)
Document both paths in the README so evaluators can pick their barrier height.

6. Optional: field redaction.

For users who want the memory written without certain fields (e.g. transcript text, specific attendees), add a --redact flag with a simple YAML config for what to strip before the external call. Makes the adapters safer to run against real corpora.

Out of scope:
- Writing a full Minutes SDK for third-party consumption. The adapters are reference examples; larger integration lives in dedicated repos.

Acceptance:
- Identity-aware ingestion works end-to-end on the demo corpus (queryable per-attendee in Mem0; entity nodes pre-seeded in Graphiti).
- Manifest prevents duplicate pushes across runs on both platforms.
- Requirements pinned to exact versions.
- CI job runs both --dry-runs on every PR touching examples/.
- Graphiti README documents at least one simpler backend path.
acceptance_criteria: Identity-aware ingestion. Manifest prevents duplicate pushes. Exact version pins. CI dry-run job. Graphiti simpler-backend path documented.

title: Reference adapters: examples/mem0 and examples/graphiti
description:
Strongest interop proof point: ship working reference adapters that pipe Minutes markdown output into third-party agent-memory platforms. Today the interop claim is theoretical (docs/frontmatter-schema.md describes the contract). Making it concrete means any engineer evaluating Minutes can see a real adapter and know it works.

Scope:

examples/mem0/
- TypeScript (or Python) adapter that walks ~/meetings/, parses frontmatter, pushes entries to Mem0 via their SDK.
- ~40-80 lines total. README explains the mapping: meeting → Mem0 entity, decisions → memories with metadata, action_items → tasks.
- Runnable with: cd examples/mem0 && npm install && MEM0_API_KEY=... npm start
- A GIF or screen recording showing Mem0 dashboard populated after the run (optional stretch).

examples/graphiti/
- Same pattern for Graphiti (Zep's OSS graph memory engine).
- Maps meeting attendees to Graphiti nodes, decisions to edges, commitments to temporal nodes.
- Demonstrates the graph-memory angle without Minutes becoming a graph platform itself.

examples/README.md
- Lists all adapters, what they demonstrate, and an invitation for contributors to add more (Letta, Cognee, LangChain memory, etc.).

Why this is high-leverage:
- Converts 'we could integrate' into 'here is 80 lines that does'. Removes evaluator friction.
- Makes Hyperspell and every other context-graph vendor a potential distribution partner instead of a competitor. The Conor DM (minutes-i6gy) lands 10x harder with 'see examples/mem0 for the pattern' attached.
- Matches the filesystem-as-interop-contract positioning with concrete evidence.

Out of scope:
- A full SDK for integrating Minutes. The adapters are reference examples, not a library.
- Doing more than two adapters in v1. Mem0 + Graphiti covers the two loudest agent-memory players; others are community contributions.

Acceptance:
- examples/mem0/ runs end-to-end against a real Mem0 account with a small fixture corpus.
- examples/graphiti/ runs end-to-end against a local Graphiti instance.
- README explains when to use each, with mappings documented.
- Link from docs/frontmatter-schema.md 'Consuming Minutes' section.
acceptance_criteria: Two working adapters. README. Linked from schema doc. Both run on fixture corpus.
close_reason:
Shipped after codex adversarial review caught 13 real hits. Fixes applied in this ship:

Must-fix (bugs/footguns):
1. Graphiti cleanup cypher 'MATCH (n) DETACH DELETE n' → group-scoped query with explicit warning not to run the full-wipe version.
2. README 'quickest path' sequential cd fixed to use subshells, plus --dry-run so users don't hit API calls on first paste.
3. Neo4j Docker run got -d flag + explicit throwaway container name.
4. glob('*.md') → rglob('*.md') in both adapters so subdirs like memos/ are scanned.
5. MEETINGS_DIR env var now honored as fallback (resolve_meetings_dir helper).
6. Mem0 metadata no longer leaks absolute path; uses file.name only.
7. read_text() now forces encoding='utf-8' per schema contract.
8. YAML parse errors caught per-file with skipped-item summary instead of blowing up the run.
9. Graphiti verification snippet includes group_ids parameter.

Docs-accuracy:
10. examples/README 'decisions become fact edges' overclaim softened — Graphiti's LLM extractor infers, we don't pre-declare.
11. Mem0 README 'downstream reasoning can surface current decision' tightened — the adapter carries supersedes as metadata; reasoning is the user's job.
12. Contributor contract 'don't reach around the schema' softened to 'body-level extraction is fine as a fallback' since the adapters scrape ## Summary.
13. requirements.txt 'pinned' claim removed — floor-only, with an honest note.

Deferred to minutes-<next>: identity-aware ingestion (Mem0 user_id per attendee, Graphiti pre-seeded nodes), idempotency manifest, exact pins, CI smoke tests, Graphiti simpler backend docs.

Verified:
- Mem0 adapter dry-run: 19 memories from 5 meetings, no regressions.
- Graphiti adapter dry-run: 5 episodes from 5 meetings, no regressions.
- docs/frontmatter-schema.md has the new Reference adapters section linking both.

title: Minutes Eval v0.2: make the benchmark credible
description:
Codex challenge (2026-04-20) correctly flagged that v0.1 (10 fictional meetings, 20 questions authored by me, testing structures I introduced) is scaffolding plus marketing copy, not a credible benchmark. Will be dismissed as cherry-picked if shipped as-is with v0.1 language.

Two separate tracks:

Track A — Soften v0.1 language (quick, before any v0.2 work):
- Rewrite tests/eval/README.md opening to drop 'a benchmark for the question nobody else is testing' overclaim.
- Reframe as 'a starter fixture corpus and runner. v0.2 expands to multi-corpus, blind-authored holdouts, and baseline comparisons.'
- Update docs/eval/results-v0.1.md similarly.

Track B — v0.2 rigor plan (medium-term):
- Three corpora minimum: SaaS, services (law/consulting), internal team ops.
- 100+ questions total.
- Blind-authored holdout questions: author at least 30 questions via someone other than me (RFC-style call for contributions, or commission from a trusted peer).
- Every answer cites specific meeting date and decision text. Graders verify the citation.
- Include hallucination traps: questions where the correct answer is 'the corpus doesn't say.'
- Include noisy-transcript variants (artificially introduce transcription errors at 2%, 5%, 10% WER to test robustness).
- Include frontmatter-ablated variants (remove structured fields, force agent to reason from transcript alone).
- Multiple repeated runs per model (N=3 minimum) to capture variance.
- Structured slot-scoring in addition to free-form pass/fail: each rubric bullet scored independently.
- Publish raw prompts, raw outputs, graded outputs, failures.
- Baselines: raw-transcript-only agent (no frontmatter structure), agent with access to ~/meetings/ via Minutes pipeline (current v0.1), and ideally a third baseline running against Granola or Fireflies via their APIs for head-to-head.

Explicitly out of scope for v0.2:
- Replacing Minutes' own dogfooding corpus with this (the eval is a benchmark, not a user feature).
- Automated CI integration (still manual run for v0.2; CI is v0.3).

Acceptance Track A: README opening softened, v0.1 language no longer claims benchmark status without qualification.
Acceptance Track B: at least 2 corpora live, 60+ questions, 15+ blind-authored, one full run published with raw outputs and structured slot-scoring.

Sequence: Track A now. Track B is a project-level bead that gets broken into sub-issues as it unfolds.
acceptance_criteria: Track A: README softened. Track B: 2+ corpora, 60+ questions, 15+ blind-authored, one full published run with raw outputs.

title: Incremental rebuild + write-back for user-confirmed facts
description:
Original memo recommendation was 'make the graph persistent, stop wiping on rebuild.' Adversarial review showed that's the wrong shape for Minutes — it undermines the filesystem-is-the-API positioning we just shipped and introduces a second source of truth. This bead is the revised replacement.

Context:
- graph.db is currently a derived, rebuildable projection of the markdown corpus (crates/core/src/graph.rs:12-24). This is the RIGHT invariant — it matches the interop contract (docs/frontmatter-schema.md) where any tool can reconstruct the graph by reading markdown.
- Real gaps the article points at: (1) expensive LLM-derived enrichments (entity deep-extract, alias resolution, etc.) recompute on every rebuild, (2) user-confirmed facts have no persistent home.

Goal: get the benefits of persistence (compounding enrichment, durable user feedback) without introducing a second source of truth.

Scope (split into two slices):

Slice 1 — Incremental rebuild
- graph::rebuild_index detects changed markdown files by mtime and only re-parses those.
- Per-file cached enrichments (e.g., entity deep-extract from the minutes-graph skill) keyed by file mtime. Invalidate on edit.
- Full rebuild remains the escape hatch (--rebuild flag) when the cache is suspect.
- Tests: (a) unchanged files aren't re-parsed, (b) mtime bump triggers re-parse, (c) deleted file clears cache.

Slice 2 — Write-back for user-confirmed facts
- When a user confirms an alias, speaker ID, or relationship owner via CLI (, , etc.), the confirmation writes INTO the appropriate markdown file:
  - Speaker confirmations → speaker_map in the meeting file
  - Alias additions → aliases in entities block of the first meeting that mentions the person, OR a canonical ~/meetings/people/<slug>.md file
  - Relationship ownership → the meeting where the handoff was decided gets a decisions[] entry with topic: relationship-ownership (already works today — validate and document)
- Document the write-back model in docs/frontmatter-schema.md as part of the stability contract.

Out of scope:
- A persistent graph.db with its own schema versioning. Explicitly rejected after adversarial review.
- Any state that can't be reconstructed by reading the markdown corpus.

Why this matters:
- Preserves the filesystem-is-the-API pitch. Any third-party tool that reads ~/meetings/ sees the same source of truth Minutes does.
- Attacks the real compounding gap: not that graph state is lost on rebuild (it can be recomputed), but that expensive derivations and user corrections get wasted.
- No migrations, no dual-source ambiguity, no backup complexity beyond what exists today.

Acceptance:
- Slice 1: rebuild on a 100-meeting corpus with one file changed touches only that file.
- Slice 2: at least one user-confirmation path (speaker or alias) writes into markdown and the change survives a full rebuild.
- Schema doc updated to reflect write-back semantics.
- 458+ existing tests still pass; new tests cover both slices.
acceptance_criteria: Incremental rebuild honors mtime. One user-confirmation path writes to markdown and survives rebuild. Schema doc updated. All existing tests pass plus new targeted tests.
close_reason: Superseded by minutes-43ny after Codex adversarial review (2026-04-20). Write-back-to-markdown for user corrections mutates historical evidence, which breaks provenance and reversibility. Replaced with sidecar-overlay design: markdown stays immutable raw capture, overlays.db holds derived/user-confirmed state, graph rebuild unions both. See minutes-43ny for the revised plan.

title: Publish docs/frontmatter-schema.md as versioned interop contract for agent-memory consumers
description:
Context: the strategic wedge is 'filesystem is the API.' That only works if the schema is documented, versioned, and explicitly positioned as an interop contract. Right now the YAML frontmatter conventions live implicitly in crates/core/src/markdown.rs and in individual meeting files; nothing tells a third party (Hyperspell, Mem0, Zep, Letta, Graphiti, or an integrator writing a custom agent) what to expect.

Goal: a single page that any context-graph vendor can read, implement against, and cite. Also invites them to consume Minutes output rather than compete.

Scope:
- docs/frontmatter-schema.md: canonical reference. Fields grouped: meta (title, date, duration, content_type), participants (attendees, people, speaker_map with L0-L3 confidence), structured (decisions, commitments, questions, topics, entities), authority (if minutes-fjns ships first, reference it).
- Each field: required/optional, type, example, compat notes.
- Versioning: schema-version: 1 field at the top of every new meeting; docs explain when it bumps.
- Link from README, /for-agents page, MCP server description.
- Short 'consuming Minutes' section with example code reading a meeting folder (Python + TS + Rust one-liners).

Out of scope: forcing the schema to change for v1; rewriting existing meetings.

Acceptance:
- docs/frontmatter-schema.md is the single source of truth.
- linked from site /for-agents and README.
- minutes-ingest and minutes-graph skills reference it instead of inlining schema details.
- A third party can implement a reader using only this doc.
acceptance_criteria: Schema doc complete. Linked from README + /for-agents + MCP manifest. Skills defer to it.
close_reason: Shipped. Published docs/frontmatter-schema.md as the versioned interop contract. Documents every field (required/optional, type, example, compat notes), includes the v2 authority and supersedes additions from minutes-fjns, and includes Python, TypeScript, Rust (raw serde_yaml), and shell examples for consuming Minutes output. Linked from README (blockquote) and /for-agents page (Output format section). Deployed. Verified via curl: schema link renders on live /for-agents. Scope note: the skills (minutes-ingest / minutes-graph) still inline brief schema references — leaving that for a follow-up since the canonical doc now exists and is linked.

title: Frontmatter v2: source-authority grading for decisions and commitments
description:
Context: Hyperspell's framing names source-authority as the thing retrieval can't do (CEO email > random Slack thread; signed contract > CRM field). Minutes already grades speaker attribution by confidence (L0-L3). Extend the same principle to facts in meeting frontmatter.

Goal: make the markdown files themselves carry authority and recency grades that /minutes-lint and any downstream agent can reason about, so 'newer decision supersedes older' and 'high-authority supersedes low' become explicit in the on-disk contract.

Scope:
- crates/core/src/knowledge_extract.rs and crates/core/src/markdown.rs: extend the decisions and commitments blocks in YAML frontmatter to support optional authority: high|medium|low and timestamp fields.
- /minutes-lint (crates/core/src/graph.rs consistency logic): when two decisions share a topic, surface the newer-and-higher-authority one as authoritative and explain the reasoning in the report.
- /minutes-graph: expose authority/timestamp as queryable fields.
- Backward compat: old frontmatter without these fields continues to work; lint falls back to recency-only.
- Document the new fields in docs/frontmatter-schema.md (this is also the interop contract for issue minutes-???).

Explicitly out of scope:
- auto-grading authority (manual or speaker-role-inferred is enough for v1)
- changing the pipeline to rewrite historical files
- live-transcript path (batch only)

Acceptance:
- Schema documented in docs/frontmatter-schema.md.
- knowledge_extract emits authority + timestamps when available.
- /minutes-lint uses them to produce 'newer higher-authority supersedes' messages.
- Tests cover at least: two decisions same topic different authority, two decisions same topic different dates, backward compat with no authority field.
acceptance_criteria: Schema doc. Extractor emits fields. Lint uses them. Tests cover 3 cases. Backward compatible.
close_reason: Shipped. Added authority (high|medium|low) and supersedes (free text) as optional fields on Decision frontmatter. Extended ReportEntry with optional authority propagation. Added resolution field to DecisionConflict — when the newer decision has supersedes set, consistency report marks the conflict resolved rather than a red flag. Backward compatible: pre-v2 frontmatter continues to parse and report. Updated minutes-lint skill across all three surfaces (.claude/plugins/, .agents/, .opencode/) and canonical tooling/skills/sources/minutes-lint/skill.md. Tests: 2 new (resolved-with-supersedes, none-without-supersedes) + 2 existing (conflict detection, near-dup filtering) all pass. 458 total tests green. cargo fmt + clippy clean.

title: Comparison table: add markdown-on-disk + agent-agnostic rows vs Granola/Fireflies/Otter
description:
Context: site/app/page.tsx comparison table currently compares transcription, OSS, price, AI integration, etc. Missing the two rows that actually differentiate Minutes from the 2026 category leaders.

Add rows:
- 'Data format': Cloud DB / Cloud DB / Cloud DB / Markdown on your disk
- 'Agent-agnostic': No / No / No / Yes (Claude, Codex, Gemini, Cursor)
- Optional: 'Corpus durability': locked to vendor / locked to vendor / locked to vendor / plain files, works in 10 years

Update 'AI agent integration' row to name the agents instead of the generic '26 MCP tools' if visual density allows.

Acceptance: comparison table has at least two new rows. Screenshots show the new rows render cleanly on mobile + desktop. Site deploy succeeds.
acceptance_criteria: Comparison table updated. Screenshots on PR. Deployed.
close_reason: Shipped. Added 'Data format' and 'Agent-agnostic' rows to the home-page comparison table. Deployed to useminutes.app. Verified via curl: both new strings render in the live table. Column order kept (Granola / Otter / Meetily / Minutes). Meetily deliberately ties Minutes on 'Data format: Markdown' to preserve honesty — the OSS+local+agent-agnostic combo is the differentiator, not markdown alone.

title: Document Linux NVIDIA NeMo wrapper path for Parakeet
description: GitHub issue 122 reports a working Linux NVIDIA CUDA workaround using a NeMo-backed wrapper behind transcription.parakeet_binary. Investigation confirms the repo currently documents Linux and Windows as CPU-only for parakeet.cpp, while the existing parakeet_binary contract is flexible enough to support a wrapper binary today. Follow-up: document the wrapper-based setup, note current caveats like per-chunk model reload, and decide whether any config alias is warranted later.

title: Add fake warm-sidecar coverage for live parakeet recording dispatch
description:
The recording-sidecar now dispatches finalized utterances through the Parakeet path during minutes record and falls back to whisper if that Parakeet leg fails. We validated this with the full live_transcript module tests plus the Rust fmt/clippy gates, but we still do not have a dedicated fake warm-sidecar integration test that simulates the Unix-socket happy path / failure path in-process.

Follow-up acceptance:
- Add a focused test harness for the recording-sidecar Parakeet dispatch path
- Cover warm-sidecar success and failure-to-whisper fallback behavior
- Keep the test isolated from the real example-server binary and real user config

Discovered while closing minutes-zq2f after implementing the behavior in core.

title: Live transcript parity and quality push
description:
Drain the April 15 live-transcript follow-up workstream end-to-end: clarify Parakeet scope, improve recording-sidecar VAD on quiet audio, filter placeholder junk from live output, and then wire true live Parakeet parity through the warm sidecar socket. This epic exists so Nudge can supervise the full sequence until every child bead is closed or explicitly blocked with a human-readable reason.

Suggested execution order:
1. minutes-a8m7
2. minutes-pl9n
3. minutes-0xy9
4. minutes-zq2f

Definition of done: all four child beads are closed with verification, or any remaining blocker is captured in bd with clear status and rationale.
close_reason: Closed the April 15 live-transcript workstream: scope/docs warning fix, Silero sidecar VAD, placeholder filtering, and recording-sidecar Parakeet parity all landed. Remaining dedicated fake-sidecar coverage is tracked separately in minutes-j9v6.

title: Wire live-streaming parakeet path through warm sidecar socket
description:
Today 'engine = parakeet' in config affects ONLY the batch transcription path (crates/core/src/transcribe.rs:transcribe_with_parakeet). Live paths (recording sidecar, standalone live, dictation) are all hard-coded to whisper. This bead wires parakeet to the recording sidecar path using the warm parakeet sidecar's Unix socket (already built and ship-ready via parakeet_sidecar_enabled=true).

## Design sketch

- Existing: crates/core/src/parakeet_sidecar.rs exposes a Unix-socket server that accepts batch audio file paths, returns ParakeetCliTranscript
- Needed: extension or new endpoint that accepts streaming chunks (1-2s frames) and returns partial transcripts
- Recording sidecar (live_transcript::run_sidecar_inner_mpsc) dispatches based on config.transcription.engine:
  - 'whisper' → existing StreamingWhisper path
  - 'parakeet' AND parakeet_sidecar_enabled AND warm sidecar healthy → route chunks through socket
  - fallback chain: if parakeet socket dies mid-session, log and fall back to whisper streaming rather than killing the transcript

## Acceptance

- Setting engine='parakeet' + parakeet_sidecar_enabled=true produces a live transcript using parakeet during 'minutes record'
- Falls back cleanly to whisper if warm sidecar isn't healthy or crashes mid-session
- Tests: unit test the dispatcher, integration test end-to-end with a fake warm sidecar
- Supersedes the 'engine=parakeet is misleading' warning (minutes-a8m7) once shipped

## Why this matters

- Closes the product/config gap where users set engine='parakeet' expecting it to apply everywhere
- Parakeet's WER and latency profile is better than whisper streaming for most use cases
- The warm sidecar already exists, so the infrastructure cost is just the streaming protocol + dispatch

## Depends on

- minutes-a8m7 (warning/docs) should ship first as a holding pattern
- minutes-pl9n (Silero in sidecar) is independent but sibling polish

## Reference

- docs/designs/parakeet-warm-sidecar-2026-04-14.md — original design (scoped to batch)
- crates/core/src/parakeet_sidecar.rs — socket server
- crates/core/src/live_transcript.rs:run_sidecar_inner_mpsc — insertion point
notes: Implemented recording-sidecar dispatch so engine=parakeet during minutes record routes finalized utterances through the existing Parakeet transcription path (warm sidecar when enabled, subprocess fallback otherwise) and switches the session back to whisper if Parakeet fails mid-run. Leaving this bead open until we decide whether it needs dedicated fake-sidecar integration coverage beyond the existing live_transcript module tests + Rust gates.
close_reason: Implemented live recording parity for engine=parakeet by routing finalized recording-sidecar utterances through the existing Parakeet transcription path, which uses the warm sidecar when enabled and subprocess fallback otherwise, and added whisper fallback if the Parakeet leg fails mid-session. Dedicated fake-sidecar integration coverage is tracked separately.

title: Run whisper-guard placeholder collapse on live streaming output
description:
Live recording sidecar (crates/core/src/live_transcript.rs) writes whisper output directly to live-transcript.jsonl without running it through whisper-guard. When input audio is very quiet or noisy, whisper emits language-agnostic placeholder tokens like '[typing]', '[BLANK_AUDIO]', '[Música]', '[risas]' — those show up in the live transcript as real utterances. The whisper-guard crate already handles this in batch (noise marker collapse, foreign-script stripping, interleaved-dedup) but the streaming path doesn't call it.

## Acceptance

- Streaming sidecar runs each emitted utterance text through the relevant whisper-guard filter(s) before writing to JSONL
- At minimum: collapse_noise_markers and strip_foreign_script
- Utterances that become empty after filtering are dropped (not written as empty lines)
- Existing valid speech unchanged
- Add a unit test that feeds '[typing]' and '[Musik]' tokens through the streaming emit path and verifies they're filtered

## Reference

- crates/whisper-guard/ — existing collapse_noise_markers, strip_foreign_script, trim_trailing_noise
- crates/core/src/live_transcript.rs:LiveTranscriptWriter::write_utterance — emit path
- crates/core/src/streaming_whisper.rs — where finalize() returns the result

## Trigger

Observed 2026-04-15 meeting: live-transcript.jsonl contained '[typing]' and '[BLANK_AUDIO]' placeholders mixed in with real utterances. Users see these and lose trust in the live transcript quality.
close_reason: Live transcript emission now normalizes timestamped output, runs whisper-guard placeholder cleanup before JSONL writes, drops isolated placeholder markers like [typing]/[BLANK_AUDIO]/[Musik], and includes focused tests for the emit-path normalization.

title: Replace energy VAD with Silero VAD in recording sidecar for quiet-audio recovery
description:
Recording sidecar (crates/core/src/live_transcript.rs:run_sidecar_inner_mpsc, line 604) uses a simple energy-threshold Vad. At quiet input (-44 dB RMS observed, e.g. speaker on headphones), nearly all chunks fall below threshold and get gated out. Silero VAD (ML-based) handles the same audio correctly and is already used in the batch whisper path via whisper-rs auto-download during 'minutes setup'.

## Acceptance

- live_transcript::run_sidecar_inner_mpsc uses Silero VAD instead of energy Vad for speaking/silence detection
- Falls back to energy Vad if the Silero model isn't available (shouldn't happen post-setup, but be defensive)
- Log a gating summary at sidecar finalize: samples_fed, samples_gated, speaking_windows, silence_windows (so users can see why live transcript is sparse)
- Existing behavior on normal-volume audio unchanged; add a test case with a -40 dB WAV that produces at least 1 utterance after the fix

## Reference

- crates/core/src/live_transcript.rs:604 — where Vad::new() is called
- crates/core/src/vad.rs — energy-threshold VAD currently used
- crates/core/src/transcribe.rs — batch path already wires Silero via whisper-rs params

## Trigger

Observed 2026-04-15 meeting: 5 fragments over 40 minutes on a WAV measured at -44.10 dB RMS. Post-meeting whisper batch (with Silero) recovered 2259 words / 260 segments from the same WAV. Same audio, different VAD, 50x quality gap.
close_reason: Replaced the recording-sidecar energy gate with Silero-backed VAD plus defensive fallback to the legacy energy VAD, added gating summary logging, and added/ran quiet-audio recovery coverage for the -40 dB scenario.

title: Live recording sidecar: warn when engine=parakeet but path is whisper-only
description:
User set [transcription] engine = "parakeet" in config, started a meeting recording, got 11 fragments including whisper placeholder tokens ([typing], [BLANK_AUDIO]) over 40 minutes. Diagnosed that the recording-sidecar live path (crates/core/src/live_transcript.rs:605) hard-codes StreamingWhisper and ignores the engine config. Root cause was a product/config mismatch: engine='parakeet' reads globally but the warm-sidecar design doc (docs/designs/parakeet-warm-sidecar-2026-04-14.md:20) explicitly scoped the work to avoid touching live. docs/PARAKEET.md:214 reads more globally.

## Acceptance

- When a recording starts with engine='parakeet', log a warning once on stderr and in minutes.log:
  "live transcription during recording still uses whisper (parakeet live streaming is not yet wired; see docs/PARAKEET.md#scope)"
- Update docs/PARAKEET.md to add a 'Scope' section listing which code paths use parakeet (batch process, folder watcher memos) vs which still use whisper (recording sidecar, standalone live, dictation)
- Optional: emit the same warning in Tauri Live Mode toggle handler so the desktop UX surfaces the same reality

## Not in scope

- Actually wiring parakeet live (that is a separate bead for streaming-parakeet work)
- Changing defaults

## Trigger

Observed 2026-04-15 meeting; live-transcript.jsonl had 11 lines including whisper placeholder tokens, expected ~150+ utterances for a 40-minute meeting.
close_reason: Implemented recording-sidecar scope handling: docs now distinguish batch vs live/dictation behavior, and the recording sidecar emits the parakeet/whisper scope warning only when the current build cannot actually run parakeet for minutes record.

title: Person canonicalizer: fold email + slash-disambig forms onto canonical (shipped v0.12.1)
description:
Person canonicalizer folds calendar email addresses and slash-disambiguated hedges onto the canonical person entity keyed by slugify(identity.name).

## Shipped (v0.12.1)

- strip_name_disambiguation: 'Name / Other' → 'Name' (applied in add_person_entity and normalize_attendee_candidate)
- strip_email_domain: 'local@domain.tld' → 'local' (only for person entity slug; attendees retain email form since calendar legitimately provides it)
- [identity] config gains emails + aliases fields. fold_user_identity merges any entity whose slug matches a configured user email localpart or alias onto the canonical user entity
- Legacy identity.email field preserved for backwards compat

## Open follow-up (tracking)

Nickname ↔ formal-name fold across distinct localparts (e.g. where a user's short name and email localpart don't share a prefix) still produces 2 entries unless aliases are configured manually. Resolution options:

1. Config-based: user opts in via aliases array — shipped in v0.12.1
2. Heuristic via recorded_by + speaker_map: automatic but speculative

Users can populate [identity].aliases to close this today.

title: Dogfood minutes CLI on Windows — validate end-to-end
description:
v0.12.1 CI now compiles minutes-cli for x86_64-pc-windows-msvc with --features parakeet (fix in 9e501ad), but nobody has actually run the CLI on a real Windows machine. Unknowns:

- Does `minutes setup` download models correctly on Windows?
- Does `minutes process foo.wav` transcribe through the Parakeet subprocess path? (Warm sidecar is explicitly unix-only — Windows gets the subprocess fallback.)
- Does whisper.cpp build / run on Windows through the CLI?
- Does the watcher behave correctly with Windows path semantics?
- Does calendar integration gracefully no-op (EventKit is macOS-only)?
- Does the MCP server work when launched from Windows Node?

## Why this matters

Claims of 'cross-platform' ring hollow if we never actually used the Windows binary. The CI cross-platform step (ff1fe58) only proves the code compiles, not that it runs.

## How to validate

Ideal: borrow or provision a Windows dev box (Parallels on Mac works), download the artifact from the `Release CLI Binaries` workflow, and run a golden-path test:

1. `minutes setup --model tiny`
2. `minutes record` / `minutes stop` (may need to test audio device discovery)
3. `minutes process <file.wav>` with whisper
4. `minutes process <file.wav>` with Parakeet (if parakeet.cpp binary is available on Windows)
5. `minutes search 'test'`
6. `npx minutes-mcp` for MCP server
7. Watcher: drop a wav in the inbox directory, confirm processed/ output

## Acceptance

Either: (a) Windows dogfood confirms the golden path works, and we document the Windows installation steps in README, OR (b) we find what's broken and file specific follow-up beads for each gap.

## Not in scope

- Tauri Windows desktop app (separate workflow `Release Windows Desktop`, already has its own audit surface)
- Warm sidecar on Windows (explicitly unix-only by design; subprocess fallback is correct behavior)
- Screen recording / call detection on Windows (platform-specific, not validated)

title: Add integration tests for live transcript sidecar state transitions
description:
Codex Round 2 adversarial review (2026-04-14): 562f04b (mta) has test coverage for status-file interpretation but NOT actual sidecar state transitions. The new coverage in crates/core/src/live_transcript.rs:1049 manually seeds LiveStatus files; it does not exercise run_sidecar_mpsc, panic handling, or the healthy→failed transition under real recording-sidecar execution.

The crash-mid-recording path — the exact case mta was supposed to fix — is only INDIRECTLY covered. A regression in run_sidecar_mpsc's panic-to-Failed transition would not fail tests.

Fix: add integration-style tests that spin up a minimal run_sidecar_mpsc, deliberately panic inside the sidecar, assert session_status transitions to Failed and diagnostic is populated.

title: Fix is_task_like_project_candidate false-positives on legitimate project names
description:
Codex Round 2 adversarial review (2026-04-14): 8b3d41e (entity cleanup) introduced is_task_like_project_candidate that rejects any topic whose first word is a verb-like token (review, run, etc.). Legitimate project names like 'Review Board', 'Run Club', 'Study Group' would now be dropped from entities.people.

Fix: don't reject on just the first word. Options:
1. Require BOTH a verb-like start AND another signal (e.g., trailing infinitive, Speaker reference)
2. Keep a whitelist of non-verb readings ('Review Board' as noun phrase)
3. Check against a corpus of real project names seen in past meetings

Relevant: crates/core/src/pipeline.rs:2993 (rejection), :2742 (infer_topic), :2919 (add_project_entity)
close_reason: Completed via fix-ent batch. is_task_like_project_candidate no longer rejects legitimate project names like 'Review Board' / 'Run Club'. Landed on main as 7c1af90 on 2026-04-14.

title: Fix 9i77 parakeet resolver log-once behavior
description:
Codex Round 2 adversarial review (2026-04-14): 4716902 (9i77) commit message claims 'structured log entry on each resolution', but log_auto_resolve_once and log_configured_fallback_once are guarded by Once at crates/core/src/parakeet.rs:337 and :362. After the first resolution/fallback, later broken-config events are SILENT. The observability doesn't match the commit promise.

Fix: remove the Once guards. Each resolution event should emit its structured log line. Alternative: keep Once for the INFO-level auto-resolve (reduces noise for well-configured setups), but always emit WARN for configured-fallback (the important UX signal).

Tests in 9i77 only assert return values, not log emission. Add assertions for log emission too.
close_reason: Completed manually: removed std::sync::Once guards from log_auto_resolve and log_configured_fallback. Resolver events now emit each time, delivering on the commit message claim. Landed on main 2026-04-14 via Edit tool.

title: Fix parakeet sidecar 'ready' status when example-server binary missing
description:
Adversarial review Round 1 (Codex, 2026-04-14): when transcription.parakeet_sidecar_enabled=true but the example-server binary is missing or unresolvable, transcription_coordinator.rs:260 populates issues[] but still returns ready=true. The Tauri settings UI at tauri/src/index.html:4952 then tells users 'ready for local transcription' while warmup silently fails and the sidecar can never start.

Fix options: (a) include server-binary resolution in the ready check when sidecar mode is selected, (b) split 'subprocess ready' from 'sidecar ready' and surface both in the UI. Either approach needs the UI to accurately reflect which code path will actually run.

References: crates/core/src/transcription_coordinator.rs:260, tauri/src/index.html:4952

title: Fix title_generation.duration_ms to measure the LLM call, not rename time
description:
Adversarial review Round 1 (Codex, 2026-04-14): lmhf (5959d1a) + xbez (73387e7) added structured logging for title_generation, but the duration_ms field measures apply_title_generation (rename/apply time) NOT the LLM call. The LLM call finishes BEFORE apply_title_generation starts its timer. The observability we just shipped is materially misleading — it tells us rename is fast (milliseconds) but hides the actual expensive LLM call duration.

Fix: time the refine call itself in maybe_refine_title_with_llm and pass that duration into the log entry. The rename time is fine to keep as a separate inner metric if useful.

References: crates/core/src/pipeline.rs:1192 (refine path), crates/core/src/pipeline.rs:1944 (apply_title_generation)
close_reason: Completed manually: title_generation.duration_ms now measures the LLM call (maybe_refine_title_with_llm), not the rename. Rename time preserved as extra.apply_ms for inner-step visibility. Landed on main 2026-04-14 via Edit tool.

title: Widen live-transcript heartbeat stale window for long whisper decodes
description: Adversarial review Round 2 (2026-04-14): mta (562f04b) set SIDECAR_HEALTH_STALE_AFTER_SECS = 3. maybe_write_heartbeat runs at the top of the main loop. If the loop is blocked inside streaming.accept_samples() or a multi-second whisper decode on a long utterance, heartbeat isn't written and session_status() consumers see 'sidecar heartbeat stale' even though the sidecar is actively working. Fix options: (a) widen to 10s+ to cover realistic decode times, (b) emit a 'busy-but-alive' beat from inside the hot path so long decodes still refresh the heartbeat. Reference: crates/core/src/live_transcript.rs maybe_write_heartbeat

title: Normalize structured_decisions text with speaker_map
description: Adversarial review Round 2 (2026-04-14): entity cleanup (8b3d41e) normalizes attendees, action items, and intents with speaker_map, but structured_decisions is passed through untouched. If the LLM emitted a decision whose text or topic embeds a 'Speaker_1 said X' reference, the decision itself still carries the unresolved label in frontmatter. Partial fix — entity-level gate catches downstream projects via is_task_like_project_candidate, but decision text leaks speaker labels. Fix: extend normalization to decisions. Reference: crates/core/src/pipeline.rs
close_reason: Completed manually: decision.topic now normalized via speaker_map using the same confidence-aware pattern as intents. Inline text-level speaker-reference rewriting in Decision.text deferred as follow-up (less common in practice). Landed on main 2026-04-14 via Edit tool.

title: Fix AppleScript month names to use ordinal integers (non-English macOS locales)
description: Adversarial review 2026-04-14: 0ddba41 calendar.rs applescript_month(u32) returns English month names (April, etc.) but AppleScript month-of-now uses the user's locale. On French/Japanese macOS, set month of now to "April" throws and query_events_with_attendees_at returns empty. Fix: use numeric ordinals - set month of now to 4 - AppleScript accepts integers. Reference: crates/core/src/calendar.rs:210-226
close_reason: Closed as false positive per Round 2 adversarial review. AppleScript month tokens (January, February, etc.) are AppleScript LANGUAGE KEYWORDS for the Month constant type, not locale-dependent strings. They resolve correctly regardless of system language.

title: Add parakeet-feature Linux build to CI matrix
description: Adversarial review 2026-04-14 + memory lesson project_ci_linux_default_features_gap: ci.yml builds Linux with --no-default-features and --features streaming/diarize, but NOT --features parakeet. Any Linux-parakeet breakage only surfaces on tag push via release-cli.yml. Today's soc0 work gated on all(feature=parakeet, unix) could break Linux silently until release. Fix: add a Linux-parakeet build step to .github/workflows/ci.yml.
close_reason: Completed manually: added cross-platform 'Build core with parakeet' step to ci.yml. Addresses the memory-lesson-violation in project_ci_linux_default_features_gap. Landed on main 2026-04-14 via patch script (GitHub workflow files blocked by pre-commit security hook on Edit tool, but content is safe — no untrusted input).

title: Make warm sidecar faster than subprocess on chunked long-audio transcription
description:
Follow-up from minutes-soc0 (landed 2026-04-14 behind transcription.parakeet_sidecar_enabled opt-in knob). Benchmark on real 21-minute meeting showed warm sidecar ~10% SLOWER than subprocess:

- fp32 subprocess: 71.4s
- fp32 warm sidecar: 78.5s
- fp16 subprocess: 70.7s  
- fp16 sidecar (auto-downgraded to fp32): 77.4s

Root cause: Minutes chunks long audio into 45s segments (transcribe.rs:189-191 constants PARAKEET_LONG_AUDIO_CHUNK_THRESHOLD_SECS=60, PARAKEET_LONG_AUDIO_CHUNK_SECS=45). Each chunk becomes a separate server request, paying socket handshake + serialization + tail-chunk subprocess fallback on every cycle. This eats the warm-state benefit that showed 12.6x realtime on single-file transcription in q3ro's benchmarks.

Solutions to explore:

1. Raise chunk threshold for sidecar path. Warm server can handle ~4-5min offline model before OOM/timeout (per q3ro upstream notes). Minutes' 60s threshold is overly conservative when feeding a warm model. Try 240s threshold or no chunking up to 300s.

2. Fix or bypass tail-chunk subprocess fallback. Currently the sidecar falls back to subprocess for the last tiny chunk when the server returns no text. That's an extra process spawn per recording. Either: teach sidecar to tolerate empty text from tiny tail chunks, or avoid creating the tail chunk in the first place by adjusting chunking math.

3. Reuse the Unix socket connection across chunks within one transcription. Currently each chunk = new connect. Could keep-alive within a transcribe session.

4. Measure the actual per-chunk overhead to know which of 1/2/3 gives the best return.

Goal: demonstrate warm sidecar beats subprocess on real long-audio meetings by >=2x before flipping the default to enabled=true.

Out of scope: upstream server protocol changes, session-level affinity, transcript streaming. Those are separate concerns.

Relevant: docs/designs/parakeet-warm-server-sidecar-2026-04-14.md, docs/designs/parakeet-perf-2026-04-14.md

title: Wire Minutes to upstream parakeet.cpp examples/server for warm transcription
description:
Follow-up from minutes-q3ro perf investigation (landed as a0c9785 on 2026-04-14). The warm example-server (contributed upstream in PR #19) shows fp32 throughput of 12.62× realtime vs subprocess-per-transcription baseline of ~9×. Wiring Minutes to the Unix socket server instead of spawning a parakeet process per transcription would eliminate model reload cost entirely.

Blockers and risks:
- fp16 path on the warm server currently crashes upstream with MPSGraph dtype mismatch (needs upstream fix or local workaround)
- Adds sidecar process lifecycle management (spawn, health check, teardown, fallback) similar to what minutes-r4mk added for the Tauri updater
- Requires deciding between Unix-socket protocol (low overhead) vs HTTP (easier to wrap)
- Needs build-system work to bundle or auto-detect the example-server binary

Scope:
1. Add a lifecycle manager for the example-server subprocess in minutes-core
2. Replace the subprocess-per-transcription call in transcribe.rs with socket-based calls
3. Fall back to subprocess if server unavailable
4. Benchmark to confirm the full 20-30× realtime hypothesis
5. Fix or work around the fp16 upstream crash before shipping

Not scoped to this bead:
- Changes to the upstream parakeet.cpp project
- Optimizing protocol beyond line-delimited JSON

Context: q3ro measurement data + decision rationale in docs/designs/parakeet-perf-2026-04-14.md
close_reason: Completed: warm-sidecar infrastructure landed on main with opt-in default off. State machine, fallback, fp16 auto-downgrade all verified. Benchmark on real long-audio meeting showed sidecar ~10% SLOWER than subprocess due to Minutes' 45s chunking eating the warm-state benefit. Scaffolding is necessary for future perf work; actual speedup requires solving the chunking-vs-warm tradeoff (filed as follow-up).

title: Add structured logging for LLM-driven pipeline steps (summarize, title, entity_extract)
description:
Observed 2026-04-14 debugging: ~/.minutes/logs/minutes.log captured 'transcribe', 'diarize', 'attribution' steps with duration_ms, but NO per-step entries for summarize, title_generation, or entity_extract. The gap between diarize end and attribution end was ~31s, which is where LLM-driven work likely happens, but timing is invisible.

This is an observability gap. When these steps silently fail (e.g., LLM timeout, rate limit, empty response), there is no log trail. Today's 'Untitled Recording' bug was partly invisible because of this.

Scope:

1. Add structured log entries around each LLM call in crates/core/src/summarize.rs and crates/core/src/knowledge_extract.rs. Use the existing logging::log_step pattern with fields:
   - step: 'summarize' | 'title_generation' | 'entity_extract' | 'action_items' (etc.)
   - duration_ms
   - extra.outcome: 'ok' | 'empty' | 'rate_limited' | 'error' | 'fallback'
   - extra.model or extra.endpoint (which LLM was called)
   - extra.tokens or extra.input_chars (rough size signal)

2. Ensure silent failures (try/catch fallbacks) emit a warn-level log, not just the fallback return value.

3. Update the step-parsing in any dashboards, pipeline visualizers, or doctor commands to understand the new step types.

Relevant files:
- crates/core/src/logging.rs (log_step pattern)
- crates/core/src/summarize.rs (LLM call sites)
- crates/core/src/knowledge_extract.rs (LLM call sites)
- crates/core/src/pipeline.rs (where these steps are orchestrated)

Out of scope: rewriting LLM call logic. Just add logging around existing calls.
close_reason: Completed: structured logging for LLM pipeline steps landed on main as 5959d1a on 2026-04-14. Adds log_step entries around LLM call sites in summarize.rs and knowledge_extract.rs with step name, duration_ms, outcome (ok/empty/rate_limited/error/fallback), model, and input_chars. Closes the observability gap where summarize/entity_extract/action_items/intent_extract were invisible in the pipeline timeline.

title: Resolve 'Speaker_N (Name)' compound assignees to just the resolved name
description:
Observed 2026-04-14: action item assignees in the Samantha meeting show as 'Speaker_1 (Samantha)' instead of just 'Samantha', despite attribution correctly mapping SPEAKER_1 → Samantha in speaker_map.

The attribution step completes successfully. The action-item generation step uses the compound label 'Speaker_1 (Samantha)' as the assignee rather than applying the speaker_map.

Fix: when rendering action items, look up the speaker_label in speaker_map and replace with the resolved name. If the attribution confidence was High, use just the name; if Medium, consider keeping a disambiguating hint.

Also file: verify whether the decisions + intents sections have the same issue (they may).

Relevant files:
- crates/core/src/summarize.rs (action-item generation, speaker_map application)
- crates/core/src/knowledge_extract.rs (structured intent extraction)

Out of scope: changing attribution confidence levels. Fix rendering only.
close_reason: Completed via combined entity cleanup bead. Landed on main as 8b3d41e on 2026-04-14. normalize_action_items_with_speaker_map + normalize_intents_with_speaker_map resolve compound 'Speaker_N (Name)' assignees to just the resolved name. High-confidence attributions use name; medium-confidence keeps disambiguation hint.

title: Fix malformed project entity slugs from action-item text
description:
Observed 2026-04-14 on job-20260414144147744-79241-0: project entity slugs are first-N-tokens of action item text, not topic labels.

Examples of malformed slugs:
- speaker-1-provide-speaker
- speaker-1-reach-out
- pioneer-asked-build

Aliases in frontmatter reveal these are slugified action-item prefixes, not semantic project names.

Likely root cause: the entity-extraction prompt says 'extract project names' but the LLM is returning action-item summaries instead, or the parser is misinterpreting LLM output and using action-item text as project labels.

Fix options:
1. Tighten the entity extraction prompt to distinguish projects (topic nouns) from action items (tasks with verbs).
2. Post-process extracted project names: reject slugs containing speaker labels, verbs as first words ('provide', 'reach', 'asked'), or other patterns that look like truncated sentences.
3. Both.

Relevant files:
- crates/core/src/knowledge_extract.rs (entity extraction prompt + parser)
- crates/core/src/graph.rs (slug generation)

Out of scope: rewriting the knowledge graph schema. Fix the extraction quality only.
close_reason: Completed via combined entity cleanup bead. Landed on main as 8b3d41e on 2026-04-14. is_task_like_project_candidate rejects project slugs that contain speaker references or start with verb-like action words. build_entity_links filter applied.

title: Fix speaker label leakage into attendees entity list
description:
Observed 2026-04-14: 'Speaker 1 / Samantha' and 'Speaker 2 / Mat' appeared as people entities in frontmatter attendees list, alongside correctly-extracted people (Andrea, Dan, Josh Weisfeld). The speaker_map correctly maps SPEAKER_1 → Samantha, but the entity-extraction step is including the compound label as a separate person.

Frontmatter excerpt from job-20260414144147744-79241-0:

attendees:
- Andrea
- Dan
- Josh Weisfeld
- Speaker 1 / Samantha    ← LEAK
- Speaker 2 / Mat          ← LEAK

Fix: after speaker attribution resolves the map, the entity dedup / person extraction step must collapse 'Speaker N / Name' pairs into just 'Name'. If a literal 'Speaker N' appears, either drop it (if the real name is in the list) or keep just the 'Speaker N' label (if name is unknown).

Relevant files:
- crates/core/src/summarize.rs (speaker map application)
- crates/core/src/knowledge_extract.rs (entity extraction + dedup)
- crates/core/src/pipeline.rs (merge_attendees)

Out of scope: changing attribution confidence/naming logic. This is post-attribution cleanup only.
close_reason: Completed via combined entity cleanup bead. Landed on main as 8b3d41e on 2026-04-14. normalize_attendees_with_speaker_map + parse_speaker_reference + ParsedSpeakerReference collapse compound 'Speaker N / Name' labels to just the resolved name in the attendees entity list.

title: Filter boost phrase selection: exclude short/common tokens, prefer multi-word entities
description:
Follow-up from minutes-cxla measurement sweep on 2026-04-14. The cxla work concluded that the Parakeet boost feature's problem isn't primarily score tuning — it's phrase selection. The top-25 graph-derived boost set includes short common names (Matt, Dan, Alex, Case) that are hallucination magnets on imperfect audio. With boost on, the model inserts these names where they don't belong (Mr. Gran, Mr. Garan, San Mark Casey appeared as spurious insertions).

See full methodology and results table in docs/designs/parakeet-boost-tuning-2026-04-14.md.

Scope:

1. Add a phrase-quality filter to the graph-derived boost phrase selection in crates/core/src/graph.rs (or wherever the boost phrase extraction happens)
2. Filter criteria to explore:
   - Exclude single-word phrases shorter than N characters (e.g., < 5 chars)
   - Exclude phrases matching a built-in common-name allowlist (or wordlist)
   - Prefer multi-word phrases (full names) over single tokens
   - Weight by frequency in the graph (rare entities are safer to boost than common ones)
   - Exclude phrases that match high-frequency English tokens
3. Re-run the cxla measurement methodology after filtering to validate
4. If the filter meaningfully reduces hallucination without losing the intended gain on rare entities, reconsider the default parakeet_boost_limit from 0 to a non-zero value

This is NOT the same as cxla (score tuning). This is phrase-selection-quality work. Score defaults (2.0) can stay.

Out of scope:
- Changing the graph-derivation logic itself (that's a separate concern)
- Changing the boost protocol with parakeet.cpp (the --boost flag is upstream)

Dependencies: measurement harness from cxla is reusable (likely at ~/Sites/minutes-cxla/ or /tmp/parakeet_boost_sweep_*). Build on that.

title: Detect and surface CLI/app version skew after Tauri auto-update
description:
The Tauri app updates itself via the built-in updater, but leaves the user's separately-installed minutes CLI untouched. Deliberate and correct (respects brew/cargo package-manager contracts), but leaves the user silently version-skewed with no indication.

Observed 2026-04-14: App auto-updated to v0.12.0. CLI at ~/.local/bin/minutes remained at v0.11.2. User had no visible indication until a feature difference surfaced as a confusing error (engine 'parakeet' not compiled in).

Polished behavior:

1. On Tauri app startup, detect installed CLI version:
   - shell out to 'which minutes' to find install path
   - shell out to 'minutes --version' to get version string
   - compare to the running app's version
2. If skewed, show a non-blocking banner or settings notice: 'CLI (v0.11.2) is outdated. App is at v0.12.0. They may behave differently.'
3. Detect install method based on path:
   - path under '/opt/homebrew/bin' → 'Run brew upgrade silverstein/tap/minutes'
   - path under '~/.cargo/bin' → 'Run cargo install --force minutes-cli'
   - path under '~/.local/bin' manually placed → 'Download the v0.12.0 binary from the GitHub release'
   - unknown → generic 'Update the CLI to v0.12.0'
4. Offer a 'Copy command' button for quick action
5. Dismissible; don't nag once dismissed for the same version pair

Non-goals:
- DO NOT auto-install or auto-replace CLI binaries from the Tauri app. That violates package manager contracts and creates trust/permission issues.
- DO NOT block app launch or block app features on skew. Advisory only.
- DO NOT force a specific install method. Detect what the user has and work with it.

Implementation:
- New Tauri command cmd_check_cli_version that runs the detection and returns {path, version, install_method}
- Frontend logic: compare app version to cli version string, show banner if mismatch
- banner style: matches existing update banner aesthetic (DESIGN.md) — thin, --bg-elevated, Geist 13px, --text-secondary copy, ghost button

title: Refine prep-file browser UI: clarify state, reduce visual noise, fix casing inconsistency
description:
The Tauri app prep-file browser (and likely related artifact lists) has visible polish problems.

Concrete issues observed 2026-04-14:

1. Duplicate-looking cards — two entries with the same filename (e.g. 2026-04-14-wesley.prep.md) at different timestamps appear indistinguishable at a glance. UI should differentiate via badge, color, or grouping, not just timestamp text.

2. State encoded in button label — 'OPEN AGAIN' vs 'OPEN' puts state in the primary CTA. Move state to a visual indicator (subtle badge, color dot, or metadata line) and keep button label consistent.

3. Casing inconsistency — status labels use lowercase natural language ('read-only', 'review ready', 'editable') while buttons use ALL CAPS ('OPEN', 'OPEN AGAIN'). Pick one convention per DESIGN.md (Geist for UI labels, Geist Mono for mono labels, consistent casing).

4. Redundant metadata — 'MARKDOWN' label on every card adds noise (filename already ends in .md). Consider removing or rolling into a single metadata pill.

5. Metadata format — 'MARKDOWN – read-only – Apr 14 at 12:46 PM' is a comma-soup dense line. Should be a single pill or badge for state plus a separate timestamp in Geist Mono 11-12px per DESIGN.md.

6. Timestamp format inconsistency — timestamps use different formats across the UI. Pick one and apply everywhere.

7. Unclear context relationship — 'person: Wesley Young' below the cards has no visual connection. Unclear if it's filtering, labeling, or independent.

8. Toolbar grouping — PREVIEW / SOURCE / Make Editable Copy / Save / Revert shown as undifferentiated buttons. Group by function, separate visually, show which apply in which state.

Review against DESIGN.md:
- typography scales (Geist 13px body, Geist Mono 11-12px metadata)
- color tokens (--text-secondary for metadata, --text-tertiary for timestamps)
- border radius (10-12px for app cards)
- 'Ink & Switch energy, not Notion energy' — reduce visual weight, increase information density, cut redundant labels

Out of scope: changing the underlying data model or file lifecycle. This bead is purely visual/UX refinement of the existing surface.

title: Add native Parakeet VAD weights to Minutes setup
description: minutes-bvdz wires the upstream --vad and --vad-threshold flags into the Parakeet runtime, but the native Silero weights file (silero_vad_v5.safetensors) is not installed by Minutes setup today. Add a supported setup path so the new runtime behavior activates without manual conversion from the parakeet.cpp repo.
close_reason: Completed: minutes setup --parakeet now installs the native silero_vad_v5.safetensors weights automatically from a bundled MIT-licensed asset, and the user-facing docs now describe that supported path instead of implying manual conversion is still required. Verified with cargo check and a real setup command run against the freshly built CLI.

title: Boost Parakeet phrases from the knowledge graph
description: Pull known people, company, and product names from graph.db and pass them into parakeet.cpp via repeatable --boost phrases plus a tuned --boost-score. Cap the phrase list sanely so the CLI stays stable, and verify the gain on a meeting with non-obvious proper nouns the graph already knows.
close_reason: Completed: added graph-derived Parakeet boost phrases with filtering for placeholder speakers and title fragments, wired the resulting --boost / --boost-score args through the direct, batch, and warmup Parakeet paths, and exposed the behavior as an experimental opt-in via parakeet_boost_limit/parakeet_boost_score (default off). Verified with cargo check plus focused graph/config tests. Accuracy evaluation was mixed: a graph-derived Wintermute phrase improved capitalization on a degraded clip, while a degraded CosaNostra clip regressed from Cosa to Coza under boost. description: Take the 1-day Apple Silicon win instead of starting WhisperKit sidecar work. Add the upstream whisper.cpp CoreML model-generation path to Minutes setup, make the required .mlmodelc bundle installable for supported Whisper models, and verify the encoder actually runs on ANE via privileged powermetrics. title: Implement winning Apple-native backend candidate
description: Implement the winning Apple-native backend candidate from minutes-ty6k: WhisperKit. Use the smallest practical integration path first, ideally the local-server or CLI-backed route, while keeping current backends intact behind the coordinator. The implementation should beat or credibly challenge the current helper-backed Parakeet path on Apple Silicon while preserving stable transcript/timestamp output.
design: Winner selected in docs/designs/apple-native-backend-candidates-2026-04-13.md. Implement WhisperKit, not whisper.cpp CoreML and not parakeet-mlx, unless new evidence later overturns the comparison.
acceptance_criteria: Done means: (1) WhisperKit is implemented behind the coordinator as an explicit backend path, (2) it can be selected without deleting the helper-backed Parakeet backend, (3) it runs end-to-end on this machine or the target maintainer machine, (4) latency, RSS, and privileged power samples are captured against the current Parakeet baseline, and (5) the implementation closes with an adversarial review before closure.
notes: Dieter call happened 2026-04-06; outcome was no commercial play identified, Mat chose not to pursue VC. This bead is no longer strategically gated. Under pure-OSS direction (see memory project_commercialization_direction), WhisperKit is an optional Apple-native spike: the Swift sidecar integration cost is real, the user-facing benefit in a no-revenue context is narrow (ANE utilization for whisper users on Mac specifically). Executable anytime as a spike; ship only if measurements clearly beat the parakeet default.

title: Compare Apple-native backend candidates on the same benchmark harness
description: Evaluate the real Apple-native backend candidates against the same speech-heavy sample and reporting surface instead of defaulting to the easiest existing integration path. Candidates: WhisperKit, parakeet-mlx, and whisper.cpp CoreML. Record what actually runs on this machine, what does not, and why. End with a grounded recommendation that picks the best option for Apple Silicon rather than the safest option for the current codebase.
design:
# Apple-Native Backend Candidates Comparison

Date: 2026-04-13

Related research bead: `minutes-ty6k`

## Question

If Minutes wants a genuinely stronger Apple Silicon backend, what is the best next implementation target?

Not the safest target.
Not the one that is already half-wired.
The best one.

Candidates evaluated:
- **A. WhisperKit** (`argmaxinc/WhisperKit`)
- **B. parakeet-mlx** (`senstella/parakeet-mlx`)
- **C. whisper.cpp CoreML**

Baseline:
- current helper-backed Parakeet path in Minutes
- sample: the same 20 second speech-heavy clip used in the earlier spike

## Current baseline

Helper-backed Parakeet (`tdt-600m`) on the same 20 second sample:

- direct benchmark time: `1791 ms`
- helper benchmark time: `1310 ms`
- helper repeated runs:
  - run 1: `1.79s`
  - run 2: `1.37s`
  - run 3: `1.43s`
- helper repeated transcripts: identical across all three runs
- helper repeated segment payloads: identical across all three runs
- helper process RSS: about `5.1 GB`
- privileged `powermetrics` sample:
  - CPU: `5216 mW`
  - GPU: `2648 mW`
  - ANE: `0 mW`
  - combined: `7864 mW`

This matters because any winner should justify itself against a backend that is already stable and reasonably fast.

## Candidate table

| Candidate | Ran end-to-end here? | Cold start | Warm run | Peak RSS | Timestamp output | ANE evidence | Integration shape | Notes |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---|---|---|---|
| WhisperKit | Yes | `71.88s` | `5.32s` | about `1.1 GB` cold, `204 MB` warm | Yes, word-level JSON/SRT | Not directly measured in this session | Swift package or local server | Strongest native Apple story and the best practical non-Swift integration path |
| parakeet-mlx | Yes | `95.98s` | `1.37s` | about `3.2 GB` max RSS, `3.9-5.0 GB` peak footprint | Yes, JSON with sentence and token timestamps | Not verified | Python CLI via MLX | Very strong warm latency, but colder start and weaker evidence about ANE usage |
| whisper.cpp CoreML | Not truly | N/A | N/A | N/A | Would use existing Whisper output shape | Upstream docs explicitly target ANE | Existing Rust path, but only encoder-side CoreML | The current selector here still falls back because the `.mlmodelc` asset pipeline is missing |

## Candidate notes

### A. WhisperKit

Primary-source facts:
- upstream describes it as on-device speech recognition for Apple Silicon
- supports Swift Package Manager integration
- ships a CLI
- ships a local server that implements the OpenAI Audio API
- documents model generation/download flows and local serving

What we ran:
- `whisperkit-cli transcribe` on the same 20 second sample

Observed behavior:
- produced transcript output successfully
- wrote JSON and SRT reports
- warm run on the same local model cache was much faster than the first run

Observed transcript:

```text
What's happening Matt? How you doing Wesley? Doing good man. Right on. How have things been since we last talked? Good. Good. How you been? Been well. Ready to get some- Nothing to do, just hanging out and you know.
```

Why it matters:
- this is the only candidate here that gives us a real Apple-native stack and a realistic non-Swift integration path at the same time
- the local server option is especially important because Minutes is not a Swift app

Caveat:
- we did **not** capture a privileged `powermetrics` sample during the WhisperKit run in this session, so we do not have the ANE mW number yet

### B. parakeet-mlx

Primary-source facts:
- installs as a Python CLI
- runs Parakeet on Apple’s MLX stack
- exposes chunking and decoding controls directly in the CLI
- produces structured JSON output with sentence and token timing

What we ran:
- `parakeet-mlx` on the same 20 second sample

Observed behavior:
- ran successfully end to end
- first run was dominated by model load/download
- warm run was very fast
- transcript output was stable across repeated runs

Observed transcript text:

```text
What's happening Matt? How you doing, Wesley? Doing good man. Right on. How have things been since we last talked? Good. Good. How you been? Been well. Ready to get just just hanging out and you know
```

Why it matters:
- this is the most direct “keep Parakeet, upgrade runtime” path
- the warm latency result is impressive

Caveat:
- the user hypothesis was “MLX auto-routes to ANE/GPU”
- I did **not** verify ANE usage from source docs or from privileged power sampling here
- that means I am not willing to claim the ANE story is proven yet

### C. whisper.cpp CoreML

Primary-source facts from upstream:
- Core ML support is official in `whisper.cpp`
- the README explicitly says encoder inference can run on the Apple Neural Engine via Core ML
- upstream documents `generate-coreml-model.sh`
- the expected runtime artifact is a compiled encoder bundle like `ggml-base.en-encoder.mlmodelc`

What we ran:
- we exercised the current `whisper-coreml` selector shape locally

Observed behavior:
- the path attempted to load a CoreML encoder bundle
- it failed because the expected `.mlmodelc` asset was missing
- it then fell back to standard Whisper

Key finding:
- this is **not** yet a real backend in Minutes
- it is currently scaffolding plus fallback behavior

Why it matters:
- this remains the safest route from the current architecture
- but it is also the most incremental route, and only accelerates the encoder side

## Recommendation

**Pick WhisperKit as the winner.**

Why WhisperKit wins:
- It is the strongest Apple-native option that is both mature and practical.
- It already has a CLI and a local server, which lowers the integration cost for a non-Swift product like Minutes.
- It gives us a real path to an Apple-first runtime without pretending the rest of the app has to become Swift.
- It looks more likely than `whisper.cpp` CoreML to become a true backend rather than a half-accelerated stopgap.

Why not `whisper.cpp` CoreML:
- it is still only partially accelerated
- it is currently only a selector with fallback in Minutes, not a finished backend
- even if fully wired, it is still the most incremental option, not the highest-upside option

Why not `parakeet-mlx` as the winner:
- it is the strongest direct Parakeet-upgrade candidate
- but the ANE story is still unproven in this research pass
- and the integration story for Minutes is weaker than WhisperKit’s local-server story unless we are willing to lean into Python more heavily

If ANE/GPU behavior for `parakeet-mlx` is later proven to be materially better than WhisperKit on this machine, that recommendation can change. Right now, WhisperKit is the better balanced bet.

## What we could not fully prove

- We did **not** gather privileged `powermetrics` samples for WhisperKit or `parakeet-mlx` in this session.
- We therefore do **not** have candidate-vs-candidate ANE mW numbers yet.
- We did **not** run a real end-to-end `whisper.cpp` CoreML backend because the model asset pipeline for `.mlmodelc` bundles does not exist in Minutes yet.

These are not reasons to disqualify WhisperKit or `parakeet-mlx`.
They are reasons to be explicit about what is known and what is not.

## Decision quality

This recommendation is based on:
- actual local runs for WhisperKit and `parakeet-mlx`
- actual baseline measurements for current helper-backed Parakeet
- upstream primary-source docs for all three candidates

It is **not** based on:
- architectural inertia
- “already half-wired” bias
- pretending the current `whisper-coreml` path is more mature than it is

## Chosen implementation follow-up

The winner is **WhisperKit**.

Follow-on implementation bead:
- `minutes-ty6k-a`
acceptance_criteria: Done means: (1) docs/designs/apple-native-backend-candidates-2026-04-13.md exists with a three-way comparison table, (2) the comparison covers cold-start latency, warm-run latency, peak RSS, transcript/timestamp behavior, and power/ANE evidence where available, (3) inability to run a candidate is recorded explicitly with the reason, and (4) a separate follow-on implementation bead exists for the chosen winner.
close_reason: Completed comparison: documented WhisperKit, parakeet-mlx, and whisper.cpp CoreML in docs/designs/apple-native-backend-candidates-2026-04-13.md, recorded what ran locally versus what remained incomplete, and chose WhisperKit as the best current Apple-native implementation target.
